pi_items.source: task.json metadata field and verified via install_gretl.sh script which embeds the dataset from POE5 (Principles of Econometrics 5th Edition) and learneconometrics.com
Verified in setup_task.sh (line 27) and export_result.sh (line 41); verifier.py also checks this exact path (line 38)

Task description explicitly requires creating 'gdp_growth = 100 * diff(log(gdp))' (line 1 of description); export_result.sh generates this variable (line 15); verifier.py searches for 'gdp_growth' in output (line 94)

Task description (lines 2-4) specifies three OLS models: Model 1 (inf ~ const + inf(-1)), Model 2 (inf ~ const + inf(-1) + gdp_growth), Model 3 (inf ~ const + inf(-1) + gdp_growth + gdp_growth(-1)); export_result.sh generates ground truth for Model 3 (line 18); verifier.py expects 3+ columns (line 105)

data_provenance.dataset_source: POE5 (Principles of Econometrics, 5th Edition) - Hill, Griffiths, Lim (2018)
data_provenance.underlying_data_source: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis FRED database

fred_series.gdp: GDPC96 - Real Gross Domestic Product (billions of chained 2005 dollars)
fred_series.inflation: CPIAUCSL - Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers

data_characteristics.financial_crisis_period: 2008:3 to 2009:3 shows negative inflation and GDP decline

economic_concept.phillips_curve: The Phillips Curve is a fundamental economic relationship showing the inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment (or economic slack). In this task, GDP growth rate is used as a measure of economic slack as an alternative to unemployment.
economic_concept.model_progression: Three progressive models test whether including lagged GDP growth improves Phillips Curve estimates: baseline (Model 1), with contemporaneous GDP growth (Model 2), and with lagged GDP growth (Model 3)

gretl_commands_verified.modeltab: Verified as real gretl command for combining multiple OLS models into a single comparison table with LaTeX output support
gretl_commands_verified.ols: Standard Ordinary Least Squares regression command in gretl
gretl_commands_verified.series_creation: Creating new variables (gdp_growth) via algebraic expressions on existing variables is standard gretl functionality

privileged_info_summary: The task uses usa.gdt, a real dataset from the Principles of Econometrics 5th Edition (POE5) collection. The underlying data comes from the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis FRED database (GDPC96 for real GDP and CPIAUCSL for inflation). The dataset contains quarterly US macroeconomic data from 1984:1 to 2009:3 (103 observations) with GDP ranging from 7285.0 to 14774.4 billion 2005 dollars and inflation ranging from -6.3% to 5.6%. The 2008-2009 financial crisis period is reflected in the data. The task requires creating a gdp_growth variable (100 * diff(log(gdp))) and estimating three OLS models with inflation as the dependent variable, demonstrating the Phillips Curve relationship with progressive model complexity. The output is a LaTeX model comparison table saved to /home/ga/Documents/gretl_output/phillips_table.tex using gretl's modeltab command.
